data.title: U.S. soldier charged after allegedly earning $400,000 betting on Maduro’s removal
data.description:
U.S. Army soldier Gannon Ken Van Dyke allegedly earned over $400,000 on Polymarket by trading with classified information about the timing of a U.S. military operation to capture Nicolás Maduro in Venezuela.

The Justice Department announced Thursday the unsealing of an indictment charging Van Dyke with "unlawful use of confidential government information for personal gain, theft of nonpublic government information, commodities fraud, wire fraud, and making an unlawful monetary transaction."

The charges arise from an alleged scheme in which Van Dyke used sensitive classified information to make wagers on Polymarket (POLYMARKET), a prediction marketplace.

As alleged in the indictment, Van Dyke participated in the planning and execution of the U.S. military operation to capture Maduro, and used his access to classified information about that operation to personally profit. 

“Today’s announcement makes clear no one is above the law, and this FBI will do whatever it takes to defend the homeland and safeguard our nation’s secrets,” said FBI Director Kash Patel. “Any clearance holders thinking of cashing in their access and knowledge for personal gain will be held accountable.”

“Van Dyke profited more than $400,000 by trading various outcomes related to Venezuela after learning of the operation because of his role as a U.S. Army soldier,"  said FBI Assistant Director in Charge James C. Barnacle Jr. 

As alleged, on or about Dec. 26, 2025, Van Dyke created a Polymarket account, funded it, and began trading on Maduro- and Venezuela-related markets. In total, Van Dyke made approximately 13 bets from Dec. 27, 2025, through the evening of Jan. 26. Those bets all took the “YES” position on “U.S. Forces in Venezuela . . . by January 31, 2026”; “Maduro out by . . . January 31, 2026”; “Will the U.S. invade Venezuela by . . . January 31,”; or “Trump invokes War Powers against Venezuela by . . . January 31,” the DOJ statement said.

Van Dyke bet a total of about $33,034 on those outcomes while in possession of classified nonpublic information about the operation.

Prediction market platform Kalshi (KALSHI) meanwhile said on Wednesday that it has suspended and  penalized three congressional candidates - from Minnesota, Texas, and Virginia - over alleged “political insider trading” tied to their campaigns.

Dear Readers: We recognize that politics often intersect with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: MaxLinear outlines $160M-$170M Q2 revenue outlook as it lifts 2026 optical data center target to $150M-$170M
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: MaxLinear (MXL) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Q1 was a strong and important start to the year, and we believe it marks the beginning of a multiyear growth phase for MaxLinear, led by our optical data center business." (Co-Founder, Chairman, CEO & President Kishore Seendripu)

  • "Based on customer orders and rising visibility of the program ramps, we are increasing our expectations for 2026 optical data center revenue to $150 million to $170 million range." (CEO Seendripu)

  • "Keystone is now ramping at multiple major hyperscale customers across both the U.S. and Asia, supporting 400G and 800G -- 800G PAM4 deployments for scale-up and scale-out applications." (CEO Seendripu)

  • "We anticipate production ramps beginning in late 2026" for Rushmore/Annapurna, with "revenue growth expected to continue strong growth through 2027." (CEO Seendripu)

  • "We have secured our first XGS-PON design win at a U.S. hyperscale data center through a Tier 1 OEM partner" and also "won USB bridge controller designs with two major hyperscalers." (CEO Seendripu)

  • "Total revenue for the first quarter was $137.2 million" and the company saw infrastructure at "approximately $63 million" alongside broadband "approximately $44 million." (CFO & Chief Corporate Strategy Officer Steven Litchfield)

Outlook

  • "We currently expect revenue in the second quarter of 2026 to be between $160 million and $170 million." (CFO Litchfield)

  • "We expect second quarter GAAP gross margin to be approximately 56% to 59% and non-GAAP gross margin to be in the range of 58% and 61% of revenue." (CFO Litchfield)

  • "We expect Q2 2026 GAAP operating expenses to be in the range of $91 million to $97 million" and "non-GAAP operating expenses to be in the range of $61 million to $66 million." (CFO Litchfield)

  • Versus the prior quarter’s positioning for Keystone, management moved from "about $100 million to $130 million in revenue in '26" (CEO Seendripu, Q4 call) to "increasing our expectations for 2026 optical data center revenue to $150 million to $170 million range" (CEO Seendripu, Q1 call).

Financial Results

  • "Total revenue for the first quarter was $137.2 million, up from $136.4 million in the previous quarter" and "up 43% from the $95.5 million in the first quarter of 2025." (CFO Litchfield)

  • "GAAP and non-GAAP gross margins for the first quarter was 57.5% and 59.5% of revenue" and "the delta ... was primarily driven by $2.6 million of acquisition-related intangible asset amortization." (CFO Litchfield)

  • "First quarter GAAP operating expenses were $96.1 million and non-GAAP operating expenses were $59.9 million" with the gap "primarily due to stock-based compensation and performance-based equity accruals of $28.5 million combined" and "acquisition-related costs and other costs of $6.5 million." (CFO Litchfield)

  • "In Q1, net cash flow used in operating activities was approximately $8.9 million" and "we exited Q1 of 2026 with approximately $89.9 million in cash, cash equivalents and restricted cash" as the "primary use of cash" was "substantial prepayment for wafers supporting rising demand" tied to "increasing order backlog in the second half of the year." (CFO Litchfield)

Q&A

  • Tore Svanberg, Stifel: asked what drove the optical DSP outlook step-up; CEO Seendripu: "we were being conservative" and now "we have very good visibility and the ramps are setting in very nicely" across "400-gig and 800-gig solutions."

  • Tore Svanberg, Stifel: asked if wafer prepayments are done and about the revolver; CFO Litchfield: "we knew we would have some working capital needs" and "it depends on how much demand continues to improve"; on the facility, "we renewed the revolver" as it "was expiring in June" and "took it up slightly."

  • Joseph Quatrochi, Wells Fargo: pressed on supply/capacity; CFO Litchfield: "there's supply constraints out there" but "we planned well for this" and "worked really closely with the partners."

  • Joseph Quatrochi, Wells Fargo: questioned margin leverage vs. the revenue step-up; CFO Litchfield: "it's a little bit of the input cost" with "wafer costs, packaging" moving up, adding "given the uncertainty out there, I think we just want to remain cautious."

  • Christopher Rolland, Susquehanna: asked if hyperscaler language implies concentration; CEO Seendripu: "It is pretty broad-based" with designs "across all the module vendors" and "we are only halfway there" on hyperscaler diversification.

  • Richard Shannon, Craig-Hallum: asked broadband trajectory and DOCSIS 4.0 timing; CEO Seendripu: "we expect our broadband business to continue to start growing from Q2" and "a big growth is coming with Ultra DOCSIS 3.1 and 4.0 into 2027" while "operators are still delayed on their network readiness."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly positive, with repeated emphasis on durability and composition of the ramp, customer concentration, supply readiness, and why margins are not stepping up faster (e.g., "what seems like is a pretty meaningful change" from Rolland; "Why wouldn't we see maybe a little bit more leverage" from Quatrochi).

  • Management: positive in prepared remarks and generally confident in Q&A, while using caution language on costs and visibility (e.g., "we are increasing our expectations" (CEO Seendripu) alongside "we just want to remain cautious" (CFO Litchfield)).

  • Versus the previous quarter, management’s stance on optical ramp visibility strengthened (Q4: "potential upside" and emphasis on lead times; Q1: "we have very good visibility" and raised 2026 optical data center revenue expectations), while analyst probing shifted toward concentration and margin leverage as revenue inflects.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • The core narrative moved from "we expect Keystone to generate about $100 million to $130 million in revenue in '26" (CEO Seendripu, Q4 call) to "increasing our expectations for 2026 optical data center revenue to $150 million to $170 million range" (CEO Seendripu, Q1 call).

  • Q4 framed capital returns and balance sheet confidence ("we repurchased $20 million worth of our common stock" (CEO Seendripu, Q4 call)); Q1 discussion pivoted to funding growth with working capital actions, including wafer prepayments as demand rises ("substantial prepayment for wafers" (CFO Litchfield)).

  • Analysts in Q1 spent more time challenging gross margin sensitivity and mix-through timing, while Q4 questions were more centered on optical market dynamics (LPO/CPO) and general growth direction.

Risks and Concerns

  • "There's supply constraints out there" and costs are moving up across "wafer costs, packaging" with caution embedded in gross margin guidance. (CFO Litchfield)

  • Cash usage was tied to scaling supply for demand: "substantial prepayment for wafers" amid "increasing order backlog in the second half of the year." (CFO Litchfield)

  • On broadband timing, management cited operator delays: "some of the operators are still delayed on their network readiness" for DOCSIS 4.0-related upgrades. (CEO Seendripu)

Final Takeaway

MaxLinear’s management described Q1 as the start of a "multiyear growth phase" led by optical data center, raised its 2026 optical data center revenue expectations to $150 million-$170 million, and guided Q2 revenue to $160 million-$170 million. In Q&A, analysts focused on the drivers behind the optical step-up, supply readiness and working-capital impacts, and why gross margin guidance remains cautious despite the sequential revenue increase, while management reiterated improved program visibility, ongoing input-cost uncertainty, and a continued push to broaden hyperscaler diversification as ramps expand into 2027.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: DeepSeek unveils new AI model, targets OpenAI and Anthropic
data.description:
• DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) has released preview versions of a new flagship AI model, a year after shaking up Silicon Valley, describing it as the most powerful open-source platform in a bid to challenge rivals like OpenAI (OPENAI) and Anthropic (ANTHRO).
  • The Chinese startup unveiled the V4 Flash and V4 Pro preview series, touting top-tier performance in coding benchmarks and big advancements in reasoning and agentic tasks.
  • They come with several architecture upgrades and optimization improvements and can operate with a million-token context length, the startup said on Hugging Face.
  • The Hangzhou-based AI startup ignited a trillion-dollar stock market selloff in 2025 with the release of the R1, an open-source model that mimics the process of human reasoning.

data.title: Matson declares $0.36 dividend and 3M share additional buyback
data.description:
• Matson (MATX) declares $0.36/share quarterly dividend, in line with previous.
  • Forward yield 0.85%
  • Payable June 4; for shareholders of record May 7; ex-div May 7.
  • "We are pleased to announce an additional three million shares to our existing share repurchase program," said Matt Cox, Matson's Chairman and Chief Executive Officer.
  • "Since we commenced our share repurchase program in August 2021, we have repurchased approximately 14.3 million shares, or approximately 33% of the then outstanding shares, for a total cost of $1.3 billion." he added.
  • See MATX Dividend Scorecard, Yield Chart, & Dividend Growth.

data.title: Enova anticipates at least 25% 2026 adjusted EPS growth while targeting >25% EPS accretion from Grasshopper
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Enova International (ENVA) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Our first quarter results marked a great start to the year" as "first quarter originations increased a healthy 33% year-over-year to nearly $2.3 billion" and "revenue increased 17% year-over-year to a record $875 million" (CEO & Director Steven Cunningham).
  • "As a result of the strong originations growth, the portfolio increased 28% year-over-year to nearly $5.3 billion" with "small business products representing 70% of our portfolio at the end of the quarter" (CEO & Director Cunningham).
  • "Credit metrics across the portfolio reflects stable or improving performance" and "the consolidated net charge-off ratio for the first quarter" fell "to 7.6%" (CEO & Director Cunningham).
  • "We are excited about our combination with Grasshopper Bank later this year" and "we expect net synergies related to the transaction to drive adjusted EPS accretion of more than 25% once the synergies are fully realized in the first 2 years post-closing" (CEO & Director Cunningham).
  • "Total company revenue of $875 million increased 17%" and "adjusted EPS... increased 30% to $3.87 per diluted share" (CFO & Treasurer Scott Cornelis).

Outlook

  • "For the second quarter of 2026, we expect total company revenue to be 15% to 20% higher year-over-year" and "we expect the total company net revenue margin for the second quarter of 2026 to be in the 55% to 60% range" (CFO & Treasurer Cornelis).
  • "With a more normalized tax rate... these expectations should lead to adjusted EPS for the second quarter of 2026, that is 20% to 25% higher than the second quarter of 2025" (CFO & Treasurer Cornelis).
  • "For the full year, we expect growth in originations compared to the full year of 2025 of around 20%" and "adjusted EPS growth of at least 25%" (CFO & Treasurer Cornelis).
  • In the prior quarter, management guided "growth in originations... of around 15%" and "adjusted EPS growth of at least 20%" for full-year 2026 (CFO & Treasurer Cornelis, Q4 2025 call).

Financial Results

  • "Total company originations during the first quarter rose 33%... to $2.3 billion" and "small business originations rose 42% year-over-year to $1.7 billion" while "consumer originations grew 10%... to $559 million" (CFO & Treasurer Cornelis).
  • "Revenue from small business lending increased 37%... to $418 million" and "revenue from our consumer businesses increased 3%... to $446 million" (CFO & Treasurer Cornelis).
  • "The consolidated net revenue margin of 60%" and "the consolidated net charge-off ratio for the first quarter of 7.6%" included "the consumer net charge-off ratio" at "14.3%" and "the small business net charge-off ratio" at "4.6%" (CFO & Treasurer Cornelis).
  • "Marketing costs increased to 22% of revenue or $189 million" and "the current quarter includes $2.7 million of onetime deal-related expenses associated with the pending Grasshopper acquisition" (CFO & Treasurer Cornelis).
  • "We ended the first quarter with approximately $1.1 billion of liquidity" and "our cost of funds for the first quarter was 8.2%" (CFO & Treasurer Cornelis).

Q&A

  • Moshe Orenbuch, TD Cowen: Asked about SMB-heavy growth and where marketing spend was incurred; CEO & Director Cunningham said "our marketing remains very efficient" and added, "I think you should expect to continue to see the consumer year-over-year growth to accelerate" while "we'll continue to opportunistically lean into marketing".
  • Moshe Orenbuch, TD Cowen: Asked about repayment trends into April; CEO & Director Cunningham said, "a few weeks into the second quarter, we're pleased with what we're seeing as it relates to the portfolio performance".
  • David Scharf, Citizens JMP: Asked whether investors should be indifferent to consumer vs. SMB mix; CEO & Director Cunningham said, "we're pretty agnostic to the mix" and "ultimately, you get back to a pretty similar ROA across the two portfolios".
  • David Scharf, Citizens JMP: Asked about gasoline spending effects; CEO & Director Cunningham said, "the proportion spending on gas is pretty small" and "it's around 2%, and we saw a slight increase".
  • David Scharf, Citizens JMP: Asked about AI-driven changes to customer acquisition; CEO & Director Cunningham said, "our marketing teams are -- have been very active in this" and "we're well underway on that".
  • William Ryan, Seaport: Asked about confidence returning to consumer LOC growth; CEO & Director Cunningham said the company "slow[ed] growth" mid-2025, then "we've reopened" and "we're back to business the way we historically had been".
  • William Ryan, Seaport: Asked about a dip in consumer yield; CFO & Treasurer Cornelis said, "more installment... has a little lower yield than the line of credit".
  • Vincent Caintic, BTIG: Asked why originations outpaced last call’s expectations; CEO & Director Cunningham said, "we saw a lot of healthy demand" and "we were able to underwrite that with our unit economics approach".
  • Vincent Caintic, BTIG: Asked about funding-market appetite/spreads; CFO & Treasurer Cornelis said the company "upsized 4 different warehouses" by "$377 million" and "we did that at the existing terms with no widening".
  • Vincent Caintic, BTIG: Asked for a Grasshopper update; CEO & Director Cunningham said, "we continue to anticipate closing the transaction during the second half of this year" and described "a pretty typical application process".
  • Kyle Joseph, Stephens: Asked about SMB competition and share; CEO & Director Cunningham said, "the SMB market is large" and "we feel like we have a lot of advantages".

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral to slightly positive, with repeated probing on marketing efficiency, credit durability, funding access, and the Grasshopper timeline (e.g., "what did you see" driving growth and questions on funding appetite and regulation).
  • Management’s tone was positive and confident in prepared remarks and steady in Q&A, emphasizing stability and control (e.g., "we're raising our outlook for the year" and "we're pretty agnostic to the mix" (CEO & Director Cunningham)).
  • Versus last quarter, management used firmer guidance language ("raising our outlook") after previously framing 2026 as "very early in the year" with expectations based on a "stable macroeconomic environment" (CEO & Director Cunningham; CFO & Treasurer Cornelis, Q4 2025 call).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Full-year 2026 targets increased from "originations... around 15%" and "adjusted EPS growth of at least 20%" (Q4 2025) to "originations... around 20%" and "adjusted EPS growth of at least 25%" (Q1 2026) (CFO & Treasurer Cornelis).
  • Portfolio mix tilted further to SMB as management cited "small business products representing 70%" of the portfolio in Q1 2026 versus "68%" at year-end 2025 (CEO & Director Cunningham).
  • Analysts’ focus shifted from post-close regulatory structure and state activity (Q4 2025) to marketing intensity, mix economics, and AI-driven customer acquisition (Q1 2026), while Grasshopper timing remained a recurring topic in both quarters.

Risks and Concerns

  • "Consumer sentiment as well as small businesses express concerns about the future impact of the recent spike in gasoline prices" (CEO & Director Cunningham).
  • Management pointed to historical experience and monitoring as mitigation: "we didn't observe material impacts to our consumer or SMB originations or credit performance" during the 2022 energy spike, and "we'll adapt if we see something different" (CEO & Director Cunningham).
  • On deal timing, management flagged regulatory-process dependency: "we remain engaged in a constructive dialogue with both the OCC and Federal Reserve" and described moving through "the typical application process" (CEO & Director Cunningham).

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 2026 as a strong start driven by 33% origination growth, record $875 million revenue, and a 7.6% consolidated net charge-off ratio, and said it raised full-year expectations to around 20% originations growth and at least 25% adjusted EPS growth. Executives also reiterated plans to close the Grasshopper Bank deal in the second half of 2026 and maintained a target of more than 25% adjusted EPS accretion from synergies within two years post-close, while emphasizing stable credit, funding-market access at existing terms, and continued willingness to lean into marketing when unit economics meet hurdles.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Cannabis selloff hits CRON, ACB, CGC, TLRY, OGI as reclassification seen limited
data.description:
Cannabis stocks gave up their early gains on Thursday as investors reassessed the narrow impact of the U.S. government’s move to reclassify FDA-approved and state-licensed marijuana as a less risky drug.

Cronos Group (CRON), Aurora Cannabis (ACB), Canopy Growth (CGC), Tilray Brands (TLRY), and OrganiGram Holdings (OGI) slumped between 7% and ‌14% in afternoon trading.

Shares of these companies had surged after Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche in an X post said the Department of Justice was "immediately rescheduling FDA-approved marijuana and state-licensed marijuana from Schedule I to Schedule III." The department is also initiating an expedited hearing to consider the broader rescheduling ​of marijuana.

But industry participants said the move stops short of a sweeping reclassification. The order applies specifically to FDA-approved ​cannabis drugs and products operating under state medical marijuana frameworks, rather than the broader market, creating uneven ⁠benefits across the sector, FundCanna CEO Adam Stettner said, according to Reuters.

Medical operators could see clearer federal alignment and potential tax relief, he ​said, while companies focused on adult-use markets remain subject to existing restrictions, including limited access to capital and regulatory fragmentation.

The move ​was narrower than investors had hoped, rescheduling medical cannabis immediately while setting a June hearing on adult use, said Todd Harrison, founding partner and chief investment officer at CB1 Capital Management, the report added.

Cannabis-focused ETFs: AdvisorShares Pure Cannabis ETF (YOLO), ETFMG Alternative Harvest ETF (MJ), Amplify Seymour Cannabis ETF (CNBS), AdvisorShares Pure US Cannabis ETF (MSOS)

data.title: Friday’s Economic Calendar
data.description:
10:00 AM Consumer Sentiment

The University of Michigan's Consumer Survey Center questions households each month on their assessment of current conditions and expectations of future conditions.

The preliminary reading was shockingly low at 47.6 for April, so forecasters see a little recovery to 48.0 in the final April report, still remarkably weak versus 53.3 in March and down about 9 percent from a year ago. Consumers remain upset about rising prices for gas and other essentials and more fearful about jobs with the energy shock from the Iran war. 

1:00 PM Baker Hughes Rig Count

The Baker Hughes North American rig count tracks weekly changes in the number of active operating oil & gas rigs.

data.title: Knowles forecasts Q2 2026 EPS of $0.28 to $0.32 as Precision Devices ramps energy order by end of Q2
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Knowles Corporation (KN) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We started 2026 with solid financial results in Q1 and great momentum entering the rest of the year." (President, CEO & Director Jeffrey Niew)

  • "We had strong organic growth in the first quarter as we delivered revenue of $153 million, up 16% year-over-year and at the high end of our guided range." (President, CEO & Director Niew)

  • "EPS of $0.27, up 50% year-over-year, exceeded the high end of our guided range and cash utilized in operations of $1 million was within our guided range." (President, CEO & Director Niew)

  • "In Q1, Medtech & Specialty Audio revenue was $68 million, up 14% year-over-year." (President, CEO & Director Niew)

  • "In the Precision Devices segment, Q1 revenues was $85 million, up 17% year-over-year with all our end markets we serve, Medtech, Defense, Industrial and Electrification growing on a year-over-year basis." (President, CEO & Director Niew)

  • "In addition to the strong shipments we saw in the first quarter, our book-to-bill in Precision Devices was very strong at 1.19." (President, CEO & Director Niew)

  • "With a very healthy backlog of existing orders, we now expect our revenue growth in 2026 to be above the high end of our target organic revenue target of 4% to 6% that we provided at our Investor Day in May of last year." (President, CEO & Director Niew)

  • "We reported first quarter revenues of $153 million, up 16% from the year ago period and at the high end of our guidance range." (Senior VP, CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer John Anderson)

Outlook

  • "For the second quarter of 2026, revenues are expected to be between $152 million and $162 million." (Senior VP, CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Anderson)

  • "We're projecting EPS to be within a range of $0.28 to $0.32 per share." (Senior VP, CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Anderson)

  • "We're projecting adjusted EBIT margin for the quarter to be within the range of 20% to 22%." (Senior VP, CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Anderson)

  • "We're projecting cash from operating activities to be within the range of $20 million to $30 million." (Senior VP, CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Anderson)

  • "We expect full year capital spending to be approximately 4% to 5% of revenues as we continue to make investments in the first half of this year associated with capacity expansion related to the large energy order we received in 2025." (Senior VP, CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Anderson)

  • "Therefore, we are projecting Medtech & Specialty Audio will grow within the 2% to 4% range for the full year 2026." (President, CEO & Director Niew)

Financial Results

  • "In the Medtech & Specialty Audio segment, Q1 revenue was $68 million, up 14% compared with the year ago period" and "Q1 margin -- gross margins were 53.5%, up 480 basis points from the year ago period." (Senior VP, CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Anderson)

  • "For full year 2026, we expect MSA gross margins to be in line with 2025 margins of 51%." (Senior VP, CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Anderson)

  • "The Precision Devices segment delivered first quarter revenues of $85 million" and "Segment gross margins were 39.2%, up 350 basis points from the first quarter of 2025." (Senior VP, CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Anderson)

  • "These improvements were partially offset by higher factory costs in our specialty film product line as we ramp up production capacity to support our $75 million-plus energy order." (Senior VP, CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Anderson)

  • "During the first quarter, we repurchased 276,000 shares at a total cost of $7.5 million." (Senior VP, CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Anderson)

  • "We exited the quarter with cash of $41 million and $131 million of borrowings under our revolving credit facility." (Senior VP, CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Anderson)

Q&A

  • Christopher Rolland, Susquehanna Financial Group, LLLP, Research Division: asked for highlights on "numerous design wins" and lifetime value; President, CEO & Director Niew responded, "the energy order... will be fully ramped up at the end of Q2" and added, "orders are up" in defense, with "strength in '27... shaping up to be even better for defense."

  • Christopher Rolland, Susquehanna Financial Group, LLLP, Research Division: asked about pricing power and gross-margin drivers; President, CEO & Director Niew said in Precision Devices, "we should lead us in that 2% to 4% in the PD business on price a year," while Senior VP, CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Anderson said PD margin expansion is expected as "we see increasing capacity utilization... in the back half of '26."

  • Will Gildea, CJS Securities, Inc.: asked on specialty film pilots timing and new pilots; President, CEO & Director Niew said, "we're due to deliver pilots on 20 different customers over the next, say, quarter" and added on the energy order, "we are on track from a ramp standpoint" and "on track from a yield standpoint."

  • Will Gildea, CJS Securities, Inc.: asked about war-related headwinds; Senior VP, CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Anderson said, "Just a little bit on input costs" including "some resin-based products, some modest increases, but not significant," while President, CEO & Director Niew added, "a lot of our customers take possession at our dock, and we're not paying for the shipping anyway."

  • Anthony Stoss, Craig-Hallum Capital Group LLC, Research Division: asked if 2027 pricing/gross margins could be higher; President, CEO & Director Niew said, "No," adding PD pricing follows "our kind of... playbook," while Senior VP, CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Anderson said, "there is some opportunity in '27 to be above '26" because "'27 margins should be similar to what the margins we exit '26 at."

  • Anthony Stoss, Craig-Hallum Capital Group LLC, Research Division: asked on remaining energy ramp hurdles; President, CEO & Director Niew said, "it's a lot of blocking and tackling" and "All the equipment is on site."

  • Anthony Stoss, Craig-Hallum Capital Group LLC, Research Division: asked about satellite exposure in RF; President, CEO & Director Niew said, "we do have some satellite business" but emphasized focus on "super, super high-performance RF filters" and added, "we don't really want to be in the commoditized portions of the market."

  • Tristan Gerra, Robert W. Baird & Co. Incorporated, Research Division: asked about utilization and energy-ramp margin impact; President, CEO & Director Niew said PD is "probably running on average in the 80% range," while Senior VP, CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Anderson added, "think of maybe 200 to 250 basis points better than we're doing today" for PD margin trajectory tied "heavily by this energy order."

  • Tristan Gerra, Robert W. Baird & Co. Incorporated, Research Division: asked about customers securing 2027 capacity; President, CEO & Director Niew said in defense there are discussions to place "a 3- or a 5-year order" and added "97% of that book-to-bill will be shipped within 12 months."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly positive, with repeated probing on durability of demand and margins, including questions on "pricing power," energy-ramp risks, and whether customers are trying to "secure capacity into '27." (Christopher Rolland, Anthony Stoss, Tristan Gerra)

  • Management tone was positive in prepared remarks and more detailed in Q&A, including "great momentum" and "confidence" language; when pressed on 2027 pricing, President, CEO & Director Niew was direct: "No." (President, CEO & Director Niew)

  • Compared with the prior quarter, both calls emphasized backlog and the specialty film/energy ramp; Q1 introduced a more explicit acceleration message: "we now expect our revenue growth in 2026 to be above the high end" of the 4% to 6% target. (President, CEO & Director Niew)

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q1 reiterated and tightened focus on the energy order timeline, saying it "will be fully ramped up at the end of Q2" and that it was "not a significant contributor in Q1," aligning with Q4’s view that the program would be "fully ramped in Q2" and weighted to the back half. (President, CEO & Director Niew)

  • Q1 emphasized defense demand visibility into 2027 more explicitly, stating "we do expect increasing demand in 2027 and beyond" and discussing potential "3- or a 5-year order" conversations; Q4 referenced defense strength but focused more on broad end-market strength and normalized channel inventory. (President, CEO & Director Niew)

  • Q1 highlighted a stronger PD ordering signal with "book-to-bill... 1.19" versus Q4’s "book-to-bill was 1.06" and added, "This marked the sixth consecutive quarter where the book-to-bill was greater than 1." (President, CEO & Director Niew)

Risks and Concerns

  • "While we delivered significant year-over-year margin improvement in the first quarter" PD results were "partially offset by higher factory costs in our specialty film product line" during the ramp, with margin recovery tied to "the second half of 2026" as volume increases. (Senior VP, CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Anderson)

  • On geopolitical and supply impacts, management cited "some resin-based products, some modest increases, but not significant" and pointed to limited transportation exposure given customer shipping terms. (Senior VP, CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Anderson; President, CEO & Director Niew)

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 as a high-end quarter on revenue with stronger-than-guided EPS and pointed to sustained demand signals in Precision Devices, including a 1.19 book-to-bill and rising defense activity. For Q2, management guided revenue of $152 million to $162 million and EPS of $0.28 to $0.32, while repeatedly tying second-half margin improvement to the specialty film energy-order ramp reaching full ramp by the end of Q2 and higher capacity utilization thereafter.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Microsoft offers buyouts to 7% of U.S. workforce amid AI shift: report
data.description:
• Microsoft (MSFT) is offering voluntary buyouts to a portion of its U.S. workforce - marking a first for the 51-year-old software giant - as the tech industry navigates significant shifts driven by the rise of artificial intelligence. According to an internal memo obtained by CNBC and Bloomberg, the company extended buyout offers to roughly 7% of its U.S. employees on Thursday.
  • Microsoft employed roughly 228,000 people as of June 2025, and of those, 125,000 were in the U.S., according to a Securities and Exchange Commission filing.
  • Those eligible for the buyouts include workers at the senior director level and below and employees whose years of employment at Microsoft and age add up to 70 or more.
  • Details for the buyouts will be disclosed to eligible employees and their managers on May 7, CNBC reported. 
  • The development comes as Meta Platforms (META) has confirmed to its employees that it will conduct job cuts next month and will close 6,000 open roles in an effort to increase efficiencies and offset heavy spending on its artificial intelligence ambitions.

data.title: Digital Realty signals 2026 core FFO of $8.00-$8.10 while scaling 1.2 GW under construction
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Digital Realty Trust (DLR) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Digital Realty got off to a record start in the first quarter of 2026" and "we continue to gain market share in our 0-1 plus interconnection product category" while also "providing needed hyperscale capacity in our greater than a megawatt category," according to "President, CEO & Director Andrew Power".
  • Power highlighted new leasing scale and mix: "We signed over $700 million of new leases in the quarter" and "a record 21% of 0-1 megawatt bookings were AI-oriented requirements," plus "116 new logos added in the quarter" (President, CEO & Director Power).
  • On hyperscale, Power said, "we signed the largest single lease in Digital Realty history this quarter, a 200-megawatt AI inference-oriented lease with a AA-rated hyperscaler in Charlotte," and added, "we launched construction on another 200-megawatt development site in Atlanta" (President, CEO & Director Power).
  • Mercier tied results to demand and balance sheet positioning: "we are raising our 2026 core FFO per share guidance range by $0.10 to $8 to $8.10 per share" and "debt to adjusted EBITDA dipped to 4.7x at quarter end" (Chief Financial Officer Matt Mercier).

Outlook

  • "We are raising our 2026 core FFO per share guidance range by $0.10 to $8 to $8.10 per share" (Chief Financial Officer Mercier).
  • "We also expect cash renewal spreads of 6.5% to 8.5%" and "power-based occupancy is still expected to improve by 50 to 100 basis points from year-end 2025" (Chief Financial Officer Mercier).
  • "Same capital cash NOI growth of 4% to 5% on a constant currency basis" and "CapEx net of partner contributions are poised to increase ... to a range of $3.5 billion to $4 billion" (Chief Financial Officer Mercier).
  • Compared with Q4 2025, the company moved from "a core FFO guidance range for the full year 2026 of $7.90 to $8 per share" to $8.00-$8.10, and from "cash renewal spreads of between 6% to 8%" to 6.5%-8.5% (Chief Financial Officer Mercier, prior-quarter transcript vs. current).

Financial Results

  • "Core FFO of $2.04 per share for the first quarter" and "same capital cash NOI growth ... increasing by 7.9% year-over-year" (Chief Financial Officer Mercier).
  • "Total backlog ... reached a new record of $1.8 billion" and "at Digital Realty's share, the backlog reached a new record of $1 billion" (Chief Financial Officer Mercier).
  • Development and returns: "we spent $910 million on development CapEx in the quarter" and "delivered 63 megawatts of new capacity, 84% of which was pre-leased" (Chief Financial Officer Mercier).
  • Pipeline expansion: "started about 464 megawatts of new data center capacity" and "increasing our total development to 1.2 gigawatts under construction"; "our gross data center pipeline under construction stood at approximately $16.5 billion" and was "nearly 80% ... situated in the Americas region" (Chief Financial Officer Mercier).

Q&A

  • Erik Rasmussen, Stifel: Asked about AI deal economics, escalators, and training vs. inference inflection; President, CEO & Director Power replied, "our hyperscale contracts are, call it, 15 years and escalators are certainly 3% or maybe even higher," and Chief Technology Officer Chris Sharp added, "demand has definitely converted from pilot to production" and "as agents come to market, it's a demand multiplier. And so that represents to us a 5x to 30x more tokens per task".
  • Frank Louthan, Raymond James: Asked about Atlanta land bank and power timing; Chief Investment Officer Gregory Wright said, "it's, call it, north of 870 acres" and "we're still working through things with the power company".
  • Matthew Niknam, Truist: Asked about longer commencement lag; Chief Financial Officer Mercier said it was "driven by what was our largest lease this quarter" and that the 200-megawatt project "will be delivering over a phased period starting next year into '28".
  • Jonathan Petersen, Jefferies: Asked what drove OpEx pressure; President, CEO & Director Power said, "it was largely a result of a low operating expense comp in the prior year same quarter" and "driven by R&M and labor largely".
  • Eric Luebchow, Wells Fargo: Asked about delays/constraints and rent vs. cost inflation; President, CEO & Director Power said, "we're still seeing market rent growth outpacing inflationary pressure in build costs" and cited "competition over supply chain, labor" and "shortages of skilled labor electricians".
  • Michael Rollins, Citi: Asked why growth slows from Q1 pace into rest of year; Chief Financial Officer Mercier cited "OpEx" ramp, "increase in our development spend," potential land, and "capital recycling".
  • Timothy Horan, Oppenheimer: Asked for inventory/runway; President, CEO & Director Power said, "just shy roughly 3 gigawatts is operating today" and "we have another 6 gigawatts that we own today" with "1.2 gigawatts" under construction.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly positive, repeatedly opening with congratulations while probing constraints and cadence, including "data center delays" and a question on why "Core FFO per share need to slow" (Eric Luebchow, Wells Fargo; Michael Rollins, Citi).
  • Management tone was positive in prepared remarks and confident in delivery, including "record start" and "raising our 2026 core FFO"; responses in Q&A stayed explanatory on cost and schedule pressures, including "competition over supply chain, labor" (President, CEO & Director Power; Chief Financial Officer Mercier).
  • Versus Q4 2025, the current call contained more specific scale disclosures around AI and hyperscale (for example, "200-megawatt" Charlotte) and a firmer near-term guidance action ("raising" the range), while Q4 framed 2026 as "establishing" the initial range (President, CEO & Director Power; Chief Financial Officer Mercier).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance shifted from "$7.90 to $8 per share" to "$8 to $8.10 per share" for 2026 core FFO, and renewal spreads from "6% to 8%" to "6.5% to 8.5%" (Chief Financial Officer Mercier, Q4 2025 vs. Q1 2026).
  • Backlog increased from "nearly $1.4 billion" at year-end to "$1.8 billion" in Q1, while development under construction rose from "769 megawatts" to "1.2 gigawatts" and pipeline value from "just over $10 billion" to "approximately $16.5 billion" (Chief Financial Officer Mercier, Q4 2025 vs. Q1 2026).
  • Balance sheet language stayed focused on leverage discipline, moving from "Leverage remained at 4.9x" to "4.7x" (Chief Financial Officer Mercier, Q4 2025 vs. Q1 2026).

Risks and Concerns

  • Power identified industry constraints: "power availability, labor and supply chain risks and community concerns" (President, CEO & Director Power).
  • On delivery and costs, management referenced "utility partners that may have delays" and "tightness on supply chains" (President, CEO & Director Power).
  • Mercier flagged macro inputs: "Given the conflict in the Middle East, energy costs and supply chain risks are once again in the spotlight," while adding, "approximately 90% of our utility expense is reimbursed by customers" and "a large majority of our electricity is hedged forward through 2026 and beyond" (Chief Financial Officer Mercier).

Final Takeaway

Digital Realty’s management described Q1 2026 as a record start driven by both enterprise and hyperscale demand, including a 200-megawatt AI inference lease in Charlotte, record 0-1 megawatt plus interconnection bookings, and a backlog that reached $1.8 billion. With leverage at 4.7x and an expanding development program, the company raised 2026 core FFO guidance to $8.00-$8.10, while acknowledging ongoing constraints in power, labor, supply chain, and local community pushback.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
